questions.question: Explain the difference between static and dynamic memory allocation in C++.
keyPoints: Static allocation: memory fixed at compile time, stored on stack, automatic lifetime
Dynamic allocation: memory determined at runtime, stored on heap, manual lifetime with new/delete
Static: faster but inflexible, Dynamic: flexible but requires explicit management

questions.question: What is a memory leak? How can it be prevented in C++?
keyPoints: Memory leak: dynamically allocated memory not freed before losing reference
Prevention: always pair new with delete, use smart pointers (unique_ptr, shared_ptr)
Also: set pointers to nullptr after deletion, avoid unnecessary allocations

questions.question: Explain the use of new and delete operators in C++ with examples.
keyPoints: new: allocates memory on heap, returns pointer, syntax: int* p = new int(5)
delete: deallocates memory, syntax: delete p
new[] and delete[] for arrays
new(nothrow) returns nullptr on failure instead of throwing exception

questions.question: What are smart pointers? Mention types and their differences.
keyPoints: Smart pointers: classes that automatically manage dynamic memory
Types: unique_ptr (exclusive ownership), shared_ptr (shared ownership with reference count), weak_ptr (non-owning reference to shared_ptr)
Prefer using smart pointers over raw pointers to prevent memory leaks

questions.question: Explain the difference between new/delete and malloc/free in C++.
keyPoints: new/delete are C++ operators, malloc/free are C functions
new calls constructor, delete calls destructor
new returns typed pointer, malloc returns void* requiring cast
new is type-safe, malloc is not
new can be overloaded, malloc cannot

questions.question: What is a dangling pointer? How can it be avoided?
keyPoints: Dangling pointer: points to memory that has been freed
Causes: deleting pointer then using it, returning local variable address
Prevention: set pointer to nullptr after delete, use smart pointers
Accessing dangling pointer causes undefined behavior

questions.question: Explain the concept of RAII in C++ and its relation to memory management.
keyPoints: RAII: Resource Acquisition Is Initialization
Resources acquired in constructor, released in destructor
Ensures automatic cleanup when object goes out of scope
Smart pointers use RAII for automatic memory management
Prevents memory leaks by tying resource lifetime to object lifetime

questions.question: Write a C++ program to demonstrate dynamic memory allocation for a 2D array. Also explain the memory deallocation process.
keyPoints: Use new to allocate array of pointers for rows
Use new[] for each row to allocate columns
Show proper initialization and display of matrix
Emphasize using delete[] for each row first, then delete[] for row pointers
Mention common mistakes like using delete instead of delete[]

questions.question: Explain stack overflow and heap overflow. Give examples of when each might occur and how to prevent them.
keyPoints: Stack overflow: stack memory exhausted, typically from recursion or large local arrays
Heap overflow: heap exhausted from too much dynamic allocation
Prevention: avoid deep recursion, use iterative solutions, allocate large data dynamically
Stack: limited size (~1-8MB), fixed direction growth
Heap: larger but fragmented, use memory-efficient algorithms

questions.question: Compare shallow copy and deep copy with respect to dynamic memory allocation. Write a C++ class demonstrating proper deep copy constructor.
keyPoints: Shallow copy: copies pointer values, both objects share same memory
Deep copy: allocates new memory and copies actual data
Default copy constructor does shallow copy - dangerous for dynamic members
Show class with dynamic member and proper copy constructor
Copy assignment operator and move semantics should also be explained
Rule of Three/Five: destructor, copy constructor, copy assignment (and move in C++11)

questions.question: Write a C++ program demonstrating the use of smart pointers (unique_ptr and shared_ptr) for automatic memory management. Compare with raw pointer usage.
keyPoints: Create class demonstrating dynamic memory
Show unique_ptr with make_unique()
Show shared_ptr with make_shared() and reference counting
Demonstrate automatic deallocation when pointers go out of scope
Compare with manual new/delete showing potential for leaks
Mention weak_ptr for breaking circular references
